Citation: Iniesta-Sepúlveda 2020, Child Psychiatry & Human Development
Effect Size: FA vs anxiety severity: r=0.47 (95% CI 0.39–0.54)[1]; child-administered measures: r=0.27 (95% CI 0.18–0.35)[1]; parent-rated FA vs child-rated anxiety: r=0.10 (95% CI −0.02–0.23), p=0.11[1]
Key Finding: Across studies, higher family accommodation correlated with greater pediatric anxiety severity (overall r≈0.47), though associations varied by informant (e.g., weaker and non-significant when parent-rated FA was paired with child-rated anxiety).[1]
Limitation: No longitudinal studies included → limited causal inference about FA maintaining anxiety over time.[1]

Citation: Shek 2025, Frontiers in Psychiatry
Key Finding: Parental anxiety predicted child anxiety across time points (supporting “parent effects”), with some evidence of reciprocity (child anxiety → parental anxiety at later waves); effects were stronger for girls and for children vs adolescents.[2]
Limitation: Self-report questionnaires may introduce social desirability/recall bias.[2]

Citation: Ask 2021, JCPP Advances
Focus: family (intergenerational transmission: genetic vs environmental/maternal effects)
Design: Prospective population cohort (genetically informed family design; MoBa)[3]
Sample N: N=26,091 children (plus parental generation/partners in design)[3]
Effect Size: Mother–child association with offspring anxiety symptoms: r=0.16; model attributes 98% (95% CI 45–112) of this association to “potential maternal effects” after accounting for genetic transmission/assortative mating.[3]
Key Finding: After accounting for assortative mating and genetic transmission, the remaining mother–child association in anxiety symptoms was largely attributed to potential maternal effects (environmental pathways), consistent with systemic transmission beyond genetics alone.[3]
Limitation: Child anxiety was mother-reported and the SCARED anxiety scale had low internal consistency (measurement error/shared-rater bias concerns).[3]

Citation: Ahmadzadeh 2023, JCPP Advances
Focus: family (maternal symptoms → child temperament risk markers)
Effect Size: Stability-linked intergenerational correlations: offspring emotionality r=0.143; shyness r=0.031; sociability r=−0.015; co-development (slope–slope) r<0.005.[4]
Key Finding: Maternal anxiety/depression symptoms showed small associations with child temperament traits (e.g., emotionality), but minimal evidence that mother and child symptoms co-developed (changes over time largely uncorrelated).[4]
Limitation: All phenotypes were mother-reported → possible shared-rater inflation of intergenerational associations.[4]

Citation: Parrigon 2016, Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology
Key Finding: Lower attachment security to fathers and restriction of maternal autonomy granting predicted which children maintained anxiety into adolescence; partner conflict explained stability in parental anxiety over time.[5]

Citation: Brumariu 2025, Development and Psychopathology
Sample N: Supportive ES: k=50, N=10,698; Nonsupportive ES: k=47, N=10,970; Elaboration: k=6, N=867[6]
Effect Size: Supportive ES r=−0.06; nonsupportive ES r=0.18; elaboration r=−0.11 (associations with child internalizing problems, birth–18 years).[6]
Key Finding: Parent emotion socialization shows measurable (though often small) associations with child internalizing problems: nonsupportive responses correlate with higher internalizing, whereas supportive/elaborative responses correlate with lower internalizing.[6]

Citation: Shannon 2021, JMIR Mental Health
Effect Size: Problematic social media use vs anxiety symptoms: r=0.348 (95% CI 0.270–0.426), p<.001[7]
Key Finding: Problematic social media use in youth shows a moderate positive association with anxiety symptoms (r≈0.35).[7]
Limitation: Evidence largely cross-sectional/self-report; causal direction unclear and bidirectionality possible.[7]

Citation: Fassi 2024, JAMA Pediatrics
Focus: digital (social media use in clinical adolescent samples)
Sample N: k=143 studies; N=1,094,890 adolescents (11% clinical samples)[8]
Effect Size: Clinical samples: time spent r=0.08; engagement r=0.12 (internalizing symptoms)[8]
Key Finding: In adolescent clinical samples, social media use showed small positive correlations with internalizing symptoms (r≈0.08–0.12), similar in magnitude to community samples.[8]
Limitation: Correlational evidence only → no causal inference about whether social media increases internalizing symptoms or vice versa.[8]

Citation: Nan 2024, Mass Communication & Society
Effect Size: Social media use vs social anxiety: r=0.14 (95% CI 0.06–0.22), p=0.001[9]
Key Finding: Across studies, social media use was significantly associated with higher social anxiety symptoms (small effect; r≈0.14).[9]
Limitation: Mostly cross-sectional; causal direction cannot be specified (25/27 cross-sectional).[9]

Citation: Yoshizawa 2025, JMIR Pediatrics and Parenting
Effect Size: Screen time vs anxiety during COVID-19: r=0.157 (95% CI 0.0994–0.214), p<.001[10]
Key Finding: During COVID-19, higher screen time correlated with higher anxiety symptoms (r≈0.16); problematic device use showed stronger correlations with anxiety than duration-only measures.[10]
Limitation: COVID-19 period-specific evidence; synthesis is correlational (key limitations not detailed in provided extract).[10]

Citation: Li 2025, British Journal of Clinical Psychology
Design: Prospective cohort (Future Proofing Study; 12-month follow-up analyses)[11]
Effect Size: Per additional hour/day screen time: cross-sectional anxiety +0.79 CAS-8 (95% CI 0.68–0.90), p<.001[11]; longitudinal anxiety at 12 months: not significant (p=0.443)[11]
Key Finding: Screen time was associated with higher anxiety cross-sectionally, but did not predict subsequent anxiety over 12 months after adjustment, suggesting weak longitudinal maintenance effects.[11]
Limitation: Screen time measured by a single self-report item; bespoke measure not validated and prone to recall bias.[11]

Citation: Tang 2021, Clinical Psychology Review
Sample N: k=35 studies (sample sizes 126–12,866; total N not mentioned)[12]
Effect Size: Anxiety: not reported; review notes longitudinal evidence for anxiety outcomes is lacking.[12]
Key Finding: Across longitudinal studies, screen time showed only small-to-very-small associations with later depressive symptoms, and evidence supporting longitudinal associations with anxiety was lacking.[12]
Limitation: No pooled numerical effect size for anxiety is provided in the available extract; calls for studies considering screen content/motives.[12]

Citation: Rienks 2025, Behaviour Research and Therapy
Effect Size: Overall effect on child anxiety: d=−0.59 (95% CI −0.92 to −0.26)[13]
Key Finding: Parent-focused interventions reduced children’s anxiety with a medium pooled effect (d≈−0.59); effective component clusters generally included behavioral elements (with possible added value from cognitive/emotional components).[13]

Citation: Jewell 2022, Journal of Affective Disorders
Focus: intervention (parent-only interventions for school-aged child anxiety)
Sample N: k=29 studies (total N not mentioned in provided extract)[14]
Key Finding: Parent-only interventions showed significant benefit vs waitlist, but not vs other active interventions; no significant effects for child-rated outcomes.[14]
Limitation: Many included studies were rated weak quality (19/29).[14]

Citation: Sigurvinsdóttir 2019, Nordic Journal of Psychiatry
Focus: intervention (family-based CBT vs individual/controls for pediatric anxiety disorders)
Sample N: k=81 studies; N=5,913 (CBT n=3,386; control n=2,527)[15]
Effect Size: Family-based CBT vs wait-list: OR=26.21 (95% CI 6.62–103.79)[15]; family-based CBT vs attention control: OR=1.88 (95% CI 0.87–4.09)[15]
Key Finding: Family-based CBT outperformed several control conditions, but evidence for superiority over attention controls (and over individual CBT) was limited/mixed; individual CBT was not superior to family-based CBT in this synthesis.[15]
Limitation: High performance-bias risk (no blinding) and family-based CBT vs attention-control evidence based on only one study.[15]

Citation: Creswell 2024, Lancet Psychiatry
Focus: intervention (digitally augmented, parent-led CBT in routine care)
Effect Size: Primary outcome (CAIS-P interference, week 26): SMD=0.01 (95% CI −0.15 to 0.17), p<0.00001 (non-inferior to usual care)[16]
Key Finding: Therapist-supported, digitally augmented, parent-led CBT (OSI) was non-inferior to usual service treatment for child anxiety interference at 26 weeks, while reducing therapist time and costs.[16]
Limitation: Unblinded trial; heavy reliance on participant-reported outcomes (parent prioritized).[16]

Citation: Dunn 2024, The Lancet Regional Health – Europe
Focus: intervention (online parent intervention to prevent child anxiety; anxious parents)
Design: Parallel-group unblinded RCT (online course vs no-intervention control)[17]
Effect Size: Child anxiety at 6 months: adjusted effect size −0.15 SD (95% CI −0.23 to −0.08), p<0.001; Cohen’s d=−0.16 (95% CI −0.23 to −0.08)[17]
Key Finding: An unguided online intervention targeting highly anxious parents reduced parent-reported child anxiety vs no intervention at 6 months (d≈−0.16), with effects maintained at longer-term follow-up in reported models.[17]
Limitation: Differential retention at 6 months (intervention 57.3% vs control 77.8%).[17]

Citation: Elfström 2025, Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry
Effect Size: Primary outcome (CSR change at 12 months): OR=0.67 (95% CI 0.30–1.48)[18]; child anxiety symptoms (secondary): d=0.35 (95% CI 0.15–0.55)[18]; subgroup (age 5–6) anxiety diagnosis OR=0.23 (95% CI 0.05–0.84)[18]
Key Finding: CPBC did not significantly improve the primary prevention outcome or reduce disorder prevalence overall at 12 months, but showed improvement on child symptom measures and possible stronger effects in younger children (5–6 years).[18]
Limitation: Open-label/lack of participant blinding (risk of bias).[18]
